    19→
    20→## 1. Levent Sagun's Work on Loss Geometry and Bias
    21→
    22→Levent Sagun (affiliated with FAIR / Meta AI Research) has published extensively on loss surface geometry, Hessian spectrum analysis, and their connections to generalization, overfitting, and bias in neural networks. His work spans from foundational Hessian analysis to fairness-oriented evaluation and graph-level bias amplification.
    23→
    24→### 1.1 Foundational Hessian Analysis
    25→
    26→#### "Eigenvalues of the Hessian in Deep Learning: Singularity and Beyond"
    27→- **Authors:** Levent Sagun, Leon Bottou, Yann LeCun
    28→- **Year:** 2016 (arXiv:1611.07476, revised 2017)
    29→- **Key findings:**
    30→  - The eigenvalue distribution of the Hessian of neural network loss functions is composed of two parts: a **bulk** concentrated around zero, and **edges** (outliers) scattered away from zero.
    31→  - The bulk reflects the degree to which the network is **over-parametrized** (more parameters than needed).
    32→  - The edge eigenvalues depend on the **structure of the input data**, not the model architecture.
    33→  - This decomposition holds both before and after training, suggesting that the loss landscape's fundamental geometry is determined by the data-model interaction from the start.
    34→
    35→#### "Empirical Analysis of the Hessian of Over-Parametrized Neural Networks"
    36→- **Authors:** Levent Sagun, Utku Evci, V. Ugur Guney, Yann Dauphin, Leon Bottou
    37→- **Year:** 2017 (arXiv:1706.04454)
    38→- **Key findings:**
    39→  - Fixing data and increasing parameters merely scales the bulk of the Hessian spectrum.
    40→  - Fixing the dimension and changing the data (e.g., adding more clusters, making data less separable) only affects the outlier eigenvalues.
    41→  - The small number of large eigenvalues can be linked to the **spectrum of the covariance matrix of gradients of model outputs** -- meaning data distribution directly shapes the curvature of the loss.
    42→  - Small-batch and large-batch gradient descent appear to converge to different basins of attraction but are in fact connected through their flat region. This challenges conventional wisdom about the relationship between batch size, minima sharpness, and generalization bias.
    43→
    44→**Relevance to our project:** The Hessian spectrum provides a potential diagnostic tool for measuring how strongly a model's loss landscape is shaped by particular data distributions. High-eigenvalue directions correspond to data-dependent features -- if a model is biased toward certain training data patterns, this should be visible in the outlier eigenvalues. This could inform a "bias signature" in the loss geometry.
    45→
    46→### 1.2 Overfitting, Generalization, and Triple Descent
    47→
    48→#### "Triple Descent and the Two Kinds of Overfitting: Where & Why Do They Appear?"
    49→- **Authors:** Stephane d'Ascoli, Levent Sagun, Giulio Biroli
    50→- **Year:** 2020 (NeurIPS 2020)
    51→- **Key findings:**
    52→  - There are two distinct kinds of overfitting in neural networks:
    53→    1. A **nonlinear peak** at N~P (number of training samples ~ number of parameters), caused by extreme sensitivity of the output function to noise and initialization. This survives even in the absence of noise and can be suppressed by regularization.
    54→    2. A **linear peak** at N~D (number of training samples ~ input dimension), solely due to overfitting the noise in labels. This forms earlier during training.
    55→  - Both peaks can co-exist, producing a "triple descent" curve.
    56→  - The relative size of the peaks is governed by the degree of nonlinearity of the activation function.
    57→
    58→**Relevance:** Understanding where overfitting occurs and why is critical for understanding when a model's outputs are driven by genuine patterns vs. noise/frequency artifacts in the training data.
    59→
    60→### 1.3 Direct Fairness and Bias Work
    61→
    62→#### "Fairness Indicators for Systematic Assessments of Visual Feature Extractors"
    63→- **Authors:** Priya Goyal, Adriana Romero Soriano, Caner Hazirbas, Levent Sagun, Nicolas Usunier
    64→- **Year:** 2022 (FAccT 2022)
    65→- **Key findings:**
    66→  - Proposed three fairness indicators for visual systems, quantifying:
    67→    1. **Harmful label associations** (e.g., associating certain demographics with negative labels)
    68→    2. **Disparity in learned representations** of social and demographic traits
    69→    3. **Biased performance on geographically diverse images**
    70→  - Uses existing publicly available datasets collected for fairness evaluations.
    71→  - Provides a systematic framework for assessing bias in feature extractors, which are upstream of many downstream tasks.
    72→
    73→#### "Networked Inequality: Preferential Attachment Bias in Graph Neural Network Link Prediction"
    74→- **Authors:** Arjun Subramonian, Levent Sagun, Yizhou Sun
    75→- **Year:** 2023 (arXiv:2309.17417; published ICML 2024)
    76→- **Key findings:**
    77→  - GNNs used for link prediction in citation, collaboration, and social networks exhibit **within-group preferential attachment bias** -- a "rich get richer" dynamic.
    78→  - Theoretically proved that GCNs with a symmetric normalized graph filter have this bias.
    79→  - Proposed a new within-group fairness metric.
    80→  - Validated findings on real-world citation, collaboration, and social networks.
    81→
    82→**Relevance to our project:** This is directly applicable. If our agent builds problem graphs and ranks nodes/edges, preferential attachment bias means popular/well-connected problems will be further amplified. Sagun's fairness metric could be adapted for our problem graph.
    83→
    84→### 1.4 Other Relevant Work by Sagun
    85→
    86→- **"ConViT: Improving Vision Transformers with Soft Convolutional Inductive Biases"** (ICML 2021) -- Explores how architectural inductive biases interact with learned representations.
    87→- **"On the Interplay Between Data Structure and Loss Function in Classification Problems"** (NeurIPS 2021, with d'Ascoli, Gabrie, Biroli) -- Studies how data structure shapes what the model learns.
    88→- **"Vision Models Are More Robust And Fair When Pretrained On Uncurated Images Without Supervision"** (2022) -- Evidence that curated datasets introduce bias, while uncurated pretraining can improve fairness.
    89→
    90→---
    91→
    92→## 2. Training Data Bias Measurement
    93→
    94→### 2.1 Frequency-Based Approaches
    95→
    96→**Token and concept frequency analysis** is the most straightforward method for detecting overrepresentation:
    97→
    98→- **Demographic frequency counting:** Statistical measurement of the distribution of demographic attributes (or any category of interest) within a dataset. Key metrics include relative frequency of each group, variance in representation, and frequency ratios. Histograms and distribution tables reveal which concepts are overrepresented or underrepresented.
    99→- **Label frequency analysis:** Examining how often particular labels co-occur with particular features or demographics. Unequal co-occurrence rates indicate representation bias.
   100→- **N-gram and token frequency:** In text corpora, measuring how often terms associated with particular groups, concepts, or solutions appear. Overrepresented tokens lead to higher parametric confidence in those concepts during generation.
   101→
   102→**Key finding (2025):** "LLMs are Frequency Pattern Learners in Natural Language Inference" (arXiv:2505.21011) demonstrated that fine-tuned LLMs exhibit significantly increased reliance on frequency bias in NLI datasets, where predicates in hypotheses occur more frequently than those in premises for positive instances. This confirms that frequency patterns in training data directly shape model behavior.
   103→
   104→### 2.2 Embedding Space Analysis
   105→
   106→#### Word Embedding Association Test (WEAT)
   107→- **Authors:** Aylin Caliskan, Joanna J. Bryson, Arvind Narayanan
   108→- **Year:** 2017 (arXiv:1608.07187)
   109→- **Methodology:**
   110→  - Inspired by the Implicit Association Test (IAT) for humans.
   111→  - Measures cosine similarity between two sets of target words (e.g., European vs. African names) and two sets of attribute words (e.g., pleasant vs. unpleasant).
   112→  - For each target word x, computes the difference in mean cosine similarity to attribute group A vs. attribute group B.
   113→  - Reports an effect size (number of standard deviations separating the two target groups) and a p-value from a permutation test.
   114→  - Demonstrated that word embeddings trained on large public corpora (Wikipedia, Google News) consistently replicate known human biases measured by the IAT.
   115→
   116→#### Sentence Embedding Association Test (SEAT)
   117→- **Authors:** Chandler May, Alex Wang, et al.
   118→- **Year:** 2019 (arXiv:1903.10561)
   119→- **Extension of WEAT to contextualized embeddings:** Target and attribute words are inserted into semantically bleached templates (e.g., "This is [WORD]"). Sentence embeddings replace word embeddings; otherwise scoring is identical to WEAT.
   120→
   121→#### Embedding Clustering and Representation Density
   122→- Clustering analysis of embedding spaces can reveal whether certain demographic groups or concepts are more tightly clustered (well-represented) vs. diffuse (underrepresented).
   123→- Representation density analysis measures the volume of embedding space occupied by different categories, revealing structural biases in learned representations.
   124→- Voxel51 (2025) demonstrated that comparing embeddings of real vs. synthetic data reveals distribution biases, with embeddings providing a direct window into what the model "sees."
   125→
   126→#### Concept Projection
   127→- A maximum-margin support vector classifier can learn a semantic property subspace (e.g., valence/pleasantness) from contextualized embeddings, onto which other embeddings can be projected to measure bias along that dimension.
   128→
   129→### 2.3 Probing Classifiers and Behavioral Testing
   130→
   131→#### Probing Classifiers
   132→- **Survey:** "Probing Classifiers: Promises, Shortcomings, and Advances" (Belinkov, 2022, Computational Linguistics, MIT Press)
   133→- **Method:** Train a separate classifier on top of a pre-trained model's internal representations to predict specific properties (e.g., gender, sentiment, syntactic features). If the probe achieves high accuracy, the model has encoded that information.
   134→- **For bias detection:** Probes can reveal whether protected attributes (gender, race, etc.) are encoded in representations even when not task-relevant, indicating learned bias.
   135→- **Limitation:** Probing classifiers can be unreliable for concept removal and detection (Ravfogel et al., NeurIPS 2022). High probe accuracy might reflect the probe's own capacity rather than the model's representations.
   136→- **Byproduct benefit:** Probing methods can produce a representation that is unaffected by the tested concept, useful for debiasing.
   137→
   138→#### Behavioral Testing
   139→- **Social Group Substitutions (SGS):** Generate text about a topic, then substitute group-identifying terms with alternatives for other social groups. Compare outputs to detect differential treatment.
   140→- **HONEST benchmark:** Measures the proportion of sentences containing potentially offensive words among LLM-generated sentences, after substituting demographic terms.
   141→- **Sentiment analysis:** Assess whether model outputs express systematically different sentiment when discussing different demographic groups or topics.
   142→
   143→### 2.4 Benchmark Suites
   144→
   145→#### BBQ (Bias Benchmark for Question Answering)
   146→- **Authors:** Alicia Parrish, Angelica Chen, et al.
   147→- **Year:** 2022 (ACL Findings, arXiv:2110.08193)
   148→- **Scope:** 58,000 unique trinary-choice questions spanning nine social dimensions (age, race, gender, etc.) relevant for U.S. English-speaking contexts.
   149→- **Design:** Each question set includes an ambiguous context (where the correct answer is "unknown") and a disambiguated context. Bias is measured by whether models default to stereotypical answers under ambiguity.
   150→- **Recent extensions (2024-2025):**
   151→  - MBBQ (Dutch, Spanish, Turkish), BharatBBQ (eight Indian languages), KoBBQ (Korean), JBBQ (Japanese), GG-BBQ (German), BasqBBQ (Basque), EsBBQ/CaBBQ (Spanish/Catalan), PBBQ (Persian), PakBBQ (English/Urdu)
   152→  - **Open-BBQ** (Liu et al., 2024; Jin et al., 2025): BBQ-style multiple-choice metrics do not reliably transfer to open-ended (generative) QA. Models produce more biased outputs in generative settings than in constrained MCQ settings.
   153→  - **Implicit BBQ** (2025, arXiv:2512.06732): Extends BBQ to detect implicit rather than explicit biases.
   154→
   155→#### WinoBias / Winogender
   156→- Minimal pair corpora based on coreference resolution tasks.
   157→- WinoBias (Zhao et al., 2018) tests gender bias using gender pronouns (he/she/they) in occupational contexts.
   158→- Limited to binary gender and English-language pronoun structures.
   159→
   160→#### Other Benchmark Suites
   161→- **CrowS-Pairs:** Measures stereotypical bias across nine categories using minimal pairs.
   162→- **StereoSet:** Measures stereotypical bias at both sentence and discourse levels.
   163→- **RealToxicityPrompts:** Measures toxicity generation across different demographic prompts.
   164→
   165→### 2.5 Bias Measurement in LLM Outputs Specifically
   166→
   167→#### Generative Output Analysis
   168→- **"Generalization Bias in Large Language Model Summarization of Scientific Research"** (Royal Society Open Science, 2025): LLMs exhibit systematic generalization bias when summarizing scientific papers, over-emphasizing common/well-documented findings and under-representing nuanced or minority findings.
   169→
   170→#### Source Framing Bias
   171→- **"Source Framing Triggers Systematic Bias in Large Language Models"** (Germani & Spitale, Science Advances, 2025):
   172→  - Tested four LLMs (OpenAI o3-mini, Deepseek Reasoner, xAI Grok 2, Mistral) on 4,800 narrative statements across 24 topics (192,000 total assessments).
   173→  - In blind conditions: over 90% inter-model agreement.
   174→  - When source framing is introduced (e.g., attributing text to "a person from China"), agreement scores dropped dramatically (from 95% to 15% in extreme cases).
   175→  - Demonstrates that LLMs are systematically biased by source attribution, with implications for any agent that processes attributed information.
   176→
   177→#### Citation Bias in LLM Outputs
   178→- **"Large Language Models Reflect Human Citation Patterns with a Heightened Citation Bias"** (Algaba et al., 2024, arXiv:2405.15739):
   179→  - Tested GPT-4, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.5 on citation recommendation tasks.
   180→  - LLMs preferentially retrieve highly cited works, amplifying the Matthew effect ("rich get richer").
   181→  - Median cited-by counts for LLM-recommended references are significantly higher than field-level medians.
   182→  - LLMs do not sample uniformly from their training distributions.
   183→- **"Who Gets Cited? Gender- and Majority-Bias in LLM-Driven Reference Selection"** (arXiv:2508.02740, 2025):
   184→  - Found persistent preference for male-authored references.
   185→  - Majority-group bias favors whichever gender is more prevalent in the candidate pool.
   186→
   187→---
   188→
   189→## 3. Bias in Agent Decision-Making
   190→
   191→### 3.1 How Training Data Bias Propagates Through Agent Reasoning Loops
   192→
   193→Agent systems that use LLMs for reasoning operate in a **think-act-observe loop**: the agent reasons about a task, takes an action, observes the result, and iterates. Bias propagates through this loop at every stage:
   194→
   195→1. **Reasoning phase:** The LLM's parametric knowledge overrepresents popular, well-documented, Western, and high-frequency concepts. When generating candidate solutions or plans, the model disproportionately surfaces overrepresented options.
   196→2. **Action selection:** If the agent ranks or scores options using the LLM, frequency bias in parametric knowledge directly influences which actions are chosen.
   197→3. **Observation interpretation:** When the agent processes feedback, it interprets results through the same biased lens, potentially discounting evidence that contradicts its parametric priors.
   198→4. **Feedback loops:** AI systems that use biased results as input data for future decision-making create a feedback loop that reinforces bias over time (ACM FAccT 2023 classification of feedback loops in automated decision-making).
   199→
   200→### 3.2 Agents Defaulting to Overrepresented Solutions
   201→
   202→- **Frequency bias as a mechanism:** "LLMs are Frequency Pattern Learners" (2025) shows that models learn frequency patterns from datasets and exploit them for inference. Fine-tuning amplifies this reliance. An agent fine-tuned on task-specific data will increasingly default to solutions that were frequent in that data.
   203→- **Parametric knowledge dominance:** Closed-book accuracy is strongly influenced by answer frequency in the pre-training data. Even when external evidence is provided, models are more accurate on answers seen during pretraining, demonstrating that parametric and external knowledge are complementary but parametric knowledge exerts a strong pull.
   204→- **Overrepresentation of popular knowledge:** LLMs overrepresent common, well-documented, Western/online topics and present those as the primary answer; niche or local details may be thin or fabricated (hallucinated). For an autonomous research agent, this means the agent will systematically favor well-known problems, methods, and frameworks over novel or underrepresented alternatives.
   205→
   206→### 3.3 Citation Bias and Literature Bias in AI-Assisted Research
   207→
   208→This is one of the most directly relevant areas for our project:
   209→
   210→- LLMs amplify existing citation biases, preferentially recommending highly cited papers (Algaba et al., 2024). The Matthew effect is heightened: already-prominent research becomes even more visible.
   211→- Gender bias compounds citation bias: male-authored papers are systematically preferred (arXiv:2508.02740, 2025).
   212→- **Implication for research agents:** An agent that builds problem graphs from literature will inherit and amplify citation network biases. Problems associated with highly cited papers will appear more central and important than they may actually be. This is structurally identical to the preferential attachment bias Sagun identified in GNN link prediction.
   213→
   214→### 3.4 Detecting When Agent Recommendations Are Frequency-Driven vs. Quality-Driven
   215→
   216→This is an area with relatively little direct research, but several approaches can be synthesized:
   217→
   218→- **Confidence calibration analysis:** If an agent's confidence on a recommendation is disproportionately high relative to the evidence provided, this may indicate parametric frequency bias rather than evidence-based reasoning. LLMs are overconfident in 84.3% of scenarios (2025 study on confidence gaps).
   219→- **Counterfactual testing:** Present the agent with the same problem framed in terms of an overrepresented concept vs. an underrepresented one. If recommendations change based on framing rather than substance, frequency bias is driving the output.
   220→- **Source ablation:** Remove or replace source attributions and measure whether rankings change (inspired by Germani & Spitale, 2025).
   221→- **Retrieval grounding comparison:** Compare agent recommendations with and without retrieval augmentation. Divergence between parametric-only and retrieval-grounded answers indicates where the model's parametric biases are strongest.
   222→
   223→---
   224→
   225→## 4. Debiasing Approaches Relevant to Our Use Case
   226→
   227→Our target: an agent that can identify when its own problem-ranking is biased by training data, and correct for it.
   228→
   229→### 4.1 Calibration Methods
   230→
   231→#### Temperature Scaling
   232→- A single temperature parameter adjusts the softmax distribution to reduce overconfidence.
   233→- Simple and fast, but less effective under data/domain shift.
   234→- **Adaptive Temperature Scaling (ATS)** (2024, EMNLP): Yields significant improvements over static temperature scaling for multiple model families.
   235→- **Selective Logit Smoothing (SLS)** (ICLR 2025): Scalar temperature scaling paired with appropriate loss functions can improve semantic calibration.
   236→
   237→#### Platt Scaling / Isotonic Regression
   238→- Classical post-hoc calibration methods that fit a logistic regression or isotonic regression on a held-out calibration set.
   239→- Not originally designed for LLMs in distractor-rich or open-ended settings; applicability to large-scale LLMs under real-world conditions remains unclear.
   240→
   241→#### Confidence Estimation
   242→- **"A Survey of Confidence Estimation and Calibration in LLMs"** (NAACL 2024): Comprehensive survey of methods.
   243→- **"Your Pre-trained LLM is Secretly an Unsupervised Confidence Calibrator"** (arXiv:2505.16690, 2025): Shows that pre-trained LLMs can be used for confidence calibration without supervised calibration data.
   244→- **Key problem:** RLHF-trained LLMs tend to exhibit overconfidence (84.3% of scenarios), potentially due to sharpened output distributions during RLHF. This means calibration is particularly important for agent systems that use RLHF-trained models.
   245→
   246→#### Relevance to Our Use Case
   247→Calibration can help the agent express appropriate uncertainty when its recommendations may be frequency-driven. If the agent's confidence is systematically higher for overrepresented topics, calibration can normalize this. However, calibration alone does not tell the agent *why* it is confident -- it just adjusts the numbers.
   248→
   249→### 4.2 Adversarial Debiasing
   250→
   251→#### Adversarial Probing and Removal
   252→- Train an adversarial classifier that tries to predict protected attributes from the model's internal representations. The model is then trained to make the adversary's task impossible, removing the biased information from representations.
   253→- Limitation: Ravfogel et al. (NeurIPS 2022) showed probing classifiers can be unreliable for concept removal, meaning adversarial removal may be incomplete.
   254→
   255→#### Adversarial Robustness Benchmarking
   256→- **"Benchmarking Adversarial Robustness to Bias Elicitation in Large Language Models"** (Machine Learning, Springer, 2025): Proposed systematic benchmarking framework that assesses LLM robustness through probing and jailbreak techniques designed to elicit biased outputs.
   257→- **Adaptive Bias-Eliciting Questions** (ETH Zurich, 2025): Framework for automatically generating questions that expose model biases.
   258→
   259→#### Relevance to Our Use Case
   260→Adversarial approaches could be used to test whether the agent's problem rankings change under adversarial perturbations. If small changes to problem descriptions cause large ranking shifts, the ranking may be fragile and frequency-dependent rather than substantively grounded.
   261→
   262→### 4.3 Multi-Agent Cross-Checking
   263→
   264→This is the most promising approach area for our use case.
   265→
   266→#### Multi-LLM Debiasing Framework
   267→- **Authors:** Deonna M. Owens, Ryan A. Rossi, Sungchul Kim, Tong Yu, Franck Dernoncourt, et al.
   268→- **Year:** 2024 (arXiv:2409.13884)
   269→- **Two approaches:**
   270→  1. **Centralized:** One primary LLM orchestrates conversation among all participating models.
   271→  2. **Decentralized:** All models communicate directly without a controlling entity.
   272→- **Key finding:** Multi-LLM framework significantly reduces bias compared to single-model baselines across multiple demographic groups.
   273→
   274→#### MADERA: Multi-Agent Debiasing with Evidence Retrieval
   275→- **Presented at:** AAAI 2024 Symposium, "Towards Fairer AI: Multi-Agent Debiasing of LLMs With Online Evidence Retrieval"
   276→- **Architecture:**
   277→  1. **Solver Agent:** Generates initial answer and reasoning chain.
   278→  2. **Judge Agent:** Scores each reasoning step for bias and contextual relevance, explains rationale for flagged bias.
   279→  3. **Rephrase Agent:** Rephrases biased reasoning (using internal knowledge in baseline mode).
   280→  4. **Search Agent:** (Enhanced mode) Retrieves web evidence when bias is detected; Rephrase Agent incorporates this evidence.
   281→- **Two modes:**
   282→  - Baseline: Solver -> Judge -> Rephrase (internal knowledge only)
   283→  - Enhanced: Solver -> Judge -> Search -> Rephrase (with external evidence grounding)
   284→
   285→#### Multi-Agent Debate Frameworks
   286→- **"Encouraging Divergent Thinking in Large Language Models through Multi-Agent Debate"** (EMNLP 2024): Multiple LLMs debate to encourage diverse perspectives.
   287→- **MAD-Fact** (arXiv:2510.22967, 2025): Multi-agent debate for long-form factuality evaluation.
   288→- **Multi-Agent-as-Judge** (arXiv:2507.21028, 2025): Multiple agents serve as evaluators, aligning with multi-dimensional human evaluation.
   289→
   290→#### Relevance to Our Use Case
   291→The MADERA architecture is highly relevant. We could adapt it: a Solver agent proposes a problem ranking, a Judge agent evaluates each ranking decision for frequency bias, a Search agent grounds judgments in external evidence, and a Rephrase agent produces a corrected ranking. The multi-agent debate approach adds a further layer where multiple models with different training data distributions can cross-check each other's biases.
   292→
   293→### 4.4 Retrieval-Augmented Approaches
   294→
   295→#### Core Mechanism
   296→RAG combines parametric memory (learned during pre-training) with non-parametric evidence (retrieved on demand). By grounding outputs in retrieved documents, RAG reduces reliance on potentially biased parametric knowledge.
   297→
   298→#### Effectiveness and Limitations
   299→- RAG reduces hallucinations caused by insufficient parametric knowledge.
   300→- However, even with accurate retrieved content, models can still produce hallucinations by generating outputs that conflict with the retrieved information.
   301→- **Mechanistic insight (ReDeEP, 2025):** Hallucinations occur when Knowledge FFNs in LLMs overemphasize parametric knowledge in the residual stream, while Copying Heads fail to effectively retain external knowledge. This suggests a structural tension between parametric and retrieved knowledge.
   302→- **MEGA-RAG (2025):** Multi-evidence guided answer refinement framework specifically designed to mitigate hallucinations through multiple evidence sources.
   303→- **RAGTruth (Niu et al., 2024):** Benchmark and evaluation protocol for hallucination detection in RAG systems.
   304→
   305→#### RAG-Specific Bias Concerns
   306→- RAG introduces new bias effects: the retrieved documents themselves may be biased, and the retrieval mechanism may have its own frequency biases (preferring documents with certain keywords, from certain sources, etc.).
   307→- Quality control of retrieved data is critical -- sources may contain counterfactual or false information.
   308→
   309→#### Relevance to Our Use Case
   310→RAG is essential for our agent. When the agent ranks problems, it should retrieve evidence (papers, datasets, benchmarks) rather than relying solely on parametric knowledge. However, the retrieval mechanism itself needs bias correction -- we need to ensure the retriever does not simply amplify the same citation biases that exist in the parametric knowledge.
   311→
   312→---
   313→
   314→## 5. Open Problems and Gaps
   315→
   316→### 5.1 Fundamental Unsolved Problems
   317→
   318→1. **No reliable method to distinguish frequency-driven vs. quality-driven recommendations in LLM agents.** Current approaches (calibration, counterfactual testing) are indirect proxies. There is no established methodology for decomposing an agent's ranking into "what it learned from data frequency" vs. "what it learned from actual quality signals."
   319→
   320→2. **Calibration methods were not designed for agentic settings.** Temperature scaling, Platt scaling, and isotonic regression were developed for classification tasks. Their applicability to multi-step reasoning chains in autonomous agents is unclear and largely untested.
   321→
   322→3. **Feedback loop dynamics in agent systems are poorly understood.** While feedback loops in recommendation systems are well-studied, feedback loops in LLM agents that iteratively refine their own reasoning are a newer phenomenon with limited formal analysis.
   323→
   324→4. **Bias benchmarks are predominantly English-centric and classification-oriented.** Despite multilingual extensions of BBQ, most bias measurement tools assume English text and multiple-choice formats. Open-ended generative settings (which are how agents actually operate) produce systematically different (often more biased) outputs.
   325→
   326→5. **Adversarial debiasing may be incomplete.** Probing classifiers are unreliable for concept removal (Ravfogel et al., 2022), meaning adversarial approaches may leave residual bias that is harder to detect.
   327→
   328→### 5.2 Gaps Specific to Our Use Case
   329→
   330→6. **No existing framework for bias-corrected problem graphs.** The intersection of graph-based problem representation and LLM bias correction is unexplored. Sagun's work on preferential attachment bias in GNNs is the closest, but it addresses link prediction, not problem ranking.
   331→
   332→7. **Self-awareness of bias in agents is nascent.** While MADERA and multi-agent debate show promise, these are external correction mechanisms. An agent that can introspect on whether its own rankings are frequency-biased -- and explain *why* -- does not yet exist.
   333→
   334→8. **Tension between parametric and retrieved knowledge is unresolved.** ReDeEP (2025) identified the mechanistic basis (Knowledge FFNs vs. Copying Heads), but practical methods for controlling this tension in agentic settings are lacking.
   335→
   336→9. **Evaluation methodology gap.** There is no established benchmark for measuring whether an agent's problem-ranking is biased by training data. We would need to construct evaluation tasks where we know the "true" importance ranking of problems and can compare it against the agent's ranking.
   337→
   338→10. **Cross-domain bias transfer.** When an agent trained on one domain (e.g., ML research) is applied to another (e.g., materials science), how do frequency biases transfer? This is unstudied.
   339→
   340→### 5.3 Opportunities for Our Hackathon Project
   341→
   342→- **Combine Sagun's Hessian spectrum analysis with agent introspection:** Use eigenvalue analysis of the loss landscape as a signal for which features/concepts the model is most sensitive to, and flag these as potential bias hotspots.
   343→- **Adapt MADERA for problem graph construction:** Replace the QA pipeline with a problem-ranking pipeline, using Judge and Search agents to evaluate and correct the Solver's rankings.
   344→- **Build on preferential attachment bias detection:** Apply Sagun's within-group fairness metric to our problem graph to detect and correct for "rich get richer" dynamics.
   345→- **Hybrid RAG + calibration:** Use retrieval to ground problem importance in evidence, and calibration to normalize confidence scores across well-represented and underrepresented problems.
   346→- **Develop a "bias decomposition" metric:** Attempt to separate frequency-driven importance from evidence-driven importance, even if imperfect, as a research contribution.
   347→
   348→---
   349→
   350→## 6. Research Notes
   351→
   352→### Papers Successfully Accessed and Summarized
   353→- Sagun et al., "Eigenvalues of the Hessian in Deep Learning" (2016) -- arXiv
   354→- Sagun et al., "Empirical Analysis of the Hessian of Over-Parametrized Neural Networks" (2017) -- arXiv
   355→- d'Ascoli, Sagun, Biroli, "Triple Descent" (NeurIPS 2020) -- proceedings
   356→- Goyal, Romero Soriano, Hazirbas, Sagun, Usunier, "Fairness Indicators" (FAccT 2022) -- ACM/arXiv
   357→- Subramonian, Sagun, Sun, "Networked Inequality" (ICML 2024) -- arXiv/PMLR
   358→- Owens et al., "A Multi-LLM Debiasing Framework" (2024) -- arXiv
   359→- MADERA framework (AAAI 2024 Symposium) -- AAAI proceedings
   360→- Algaba et al., "LLMs Reflect Human Citation Patterns" (2024) -- arXiv
   361→- Germani & Spitale, "Source Framing" (Science Advances, 2025)
   362→- Caliskan et al., WEAT (2017) -- arXiv
   363→- May et al., SEAT (2019) -- arXiv
   364→- Parrish et al., BBQ (ACL 2022) -- arXiv
   365→- Belinkov, "Probing Classifiers" (Computational Linguistics, 2022) -- MIT Press
   366→
   367→### Limitations and Caveats
   368→- **Full-text access:** Most papers were accessed via arXiv or open-access venues. Some Springer and ACM papers could only be accessed via abstracts and summaries, not full text.
   369→- **Recency bias in this review:** The search naturally surfaces recent (2024-2025) work more readily. Older foundational work (e.g., Bolukbasi et al., 2016 on debiasing word embeddings; Bender et al., 2021 "Stochastic Parrots") is not covered in depth here but is relevant background.
   370→- **Rapidly evolving field:** LLM agent bias is a fast-moving area. Papers from late 2025 and early 2026 may contain significant advances not captured here.
   371→- **Conflicting findings on calibration:** There is disagreement about whether classical calibration methods (temperature/Platt scaling) are effective for modern LLMs in real-world settings. Some papers report significant improvements; others find them inadequate for distractor-rich or open-ended scenarios.
   372→- **MADERA details:** The MADERA paper was accessed via AAAI proceedings but the full experimental results were only partially extractable from the available PDF. The framework architecture is well-documented but quantitative results may need verification from the full paper.
   373→- **Sagun's most recent work (2024-2026):** The search did not surface publications by Sagun from 2024 onward. He may have more recent work that was not indexed or was behind paywalls.
   374→
   375→### Key Sources for Further Reading
   376→- Sagun's Google Scholar profile: https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=-iPZaBcAAAAJ
   377→- Sagun's GitHub (Hessian computation tools): https://github.com/leventsagun/hessian-for-basicDL
   378→- "Bias and Fairness in Large Language Models: A Survey" (Gallegos et al., Computational Linguistics, MIT Press, 2024) -- comprehensive survey covering the full landscape
   379→- "Bias in Large Language Models: Origin, Evaluation, and Mitigation" (arXiv:2411.10915, 2024) -- categorizes mitigation into pre-model, intra-model, and post-model techniques
   380→- "Towards Trustworthy LLMs: A Review on Debiasing and Dehallucinating" (Artificial Intelligence Review, Springer, 2024) -- covers debiasing and hallucination mitigation together
   381→

# Analysis: 0x8004A169FB4a3325136EB29fA0ceB6D2e539a432

## Type
**Smart Contract** (not an EOA)

## Contract Details

**Name:** 8004: Identity Registry

**Pattern:** ERC-1967 Transparent Proxy pattern

**Implementation Address:** 0x7274e874ca62410a93bd8bf61c69d8045e399c02 (IdentityRegistryUpgradeable)

## Key Information

The contract is an upgradeable proxy that delegates calls to an implementation contract. The proxy uses standardized ERC-1967 storage slots to store the implementation address at `0x360894a13ba1a3210667c828492db98dca3e2076cc3735a920a3ca505d382bbc`.

**Compiler:** Solidity v0.8.24 (with optimization, 200 runs)

**Associated Token:** AgentIdentity (AGENT) - deployed at this address

## Primary Functions (from transaction history)
- `register()` - Register identities
- `setAgentUri()` - Set agent URI metadata

The contract has processed over 15,445 transactions since deployment approximately 49 days ago. It appears designed for identity registration and management on Ethereum mainnet.

# ERC-8004 Specification Summary

Based on the provided documentation, here's what is and isn't available:

## What IS Included

The proposal contains **complete interface specifications** for three registries:

**Identity Registry** (ERC-721 based):
- `register()` function with optional metadata
- `setAgentURI()` and `setAgentWallet()` with EIP-712 signature verification
- `getMetadata()` and `setMetadata()` for on-chain agent data

**Reputation Registry**:
- `giveFeedback()` for submitting feedback signals
- `revokeFeedback()` and `appendResponse()` for feedback management
- Read functions like `getSummary()`, `readFeedback()`, and `readAllFeedback()`

**Validation Registry**:
- `validationRequest()` for requesting validator verification
- `validationResponse()` for validators to submit results
- Status queries via `getValidationStatus()` and `getSummary()`

## What IS NOT Included

The document explicitly **does not provide**:
- **No deployed contract addresses** on any blockchain
- **No full Solidity source code** (only function signatures)
- **No deployment examples or scripts**
- **No usage examples** with concrete parameters
- **No information about payment mechanisms** (noted as "orthogonal")

The proposal states it was "Created 2025-08-13" and remains in **Draft status**, meaning implementation details have not been finalized or deployed.

# Contract Analysis: 8004 Reputation Registry

## Is This a Contract?
**Yes**, this is a smart contract deployed on Ethereum Mainnet.

## Contract Details

**Contract Name:** ERC1967Proxy (wrapper for ReputationRegistryUpgradeable)

**Address:** 0x8004BAa17C55a88189AE136b182e5fdA19dE9b63

**Type:** Upgradeable Proxy Contract (ERC-1967 standard)

**Implementation Address:** 0x16e0fa7f7c56b9a767e34b192b51f921be31da34

## Key Function Signatures

The proxy contract implements the ERC-1967 standard with these core functions:

- `constructor(address implementation, bytes memory _data)` - Initializes proxy with implementation
- `_implementation()` - Returns current implementation address
- `fallback()` - Delegates calls to implementation via delegatecall

## Notable Details

The contract uses OpenZeppelin's standardized proxy pattern with storage slots at:
- **Implementation slot:** `0x360894a13ba1a3210667c828492db98dca3e2076cc3735a920a3ca505d382bbc`

The contract was created 49 days ago and has processed over 2,459 transactions. It currently holds zero ETH and functions as a reputation registry system with an upgradeable implementation pattern.

1→# Self-Training, Verifiability, and Agent-Ready Problem Classification
     2→
     3→## Karpathy's Key Positions (2024-2026)
     4→
     5→### RLVR as the paradigm shift
     6→In his [2025 LLM Year in Review](https://karpathy.bearblog.dev/year-in-review-2025/), Karpathy identifies Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) as the single most important development. Train LLMs against automatically verifiable signals (math correctness, code execution) rather than human preference labels. OpenAI's o1/o3, DeepSeek-R1 all use this approach.
     7→
     8→### Critique of RL for LLMs
     9→On the [Dwarkesh Patel podcast (Oct 2025)](https://www.dwarkesh.com/p/andrej-karpathy): calls RL "sucking supervision through a straw" — run a long reasoning trace, get a single binary signal (right/wrong), broadcast backward across every token. This incorrectly upweights mistakes and dead ends that happened to precede a correct answer.
    10→
    11→### The self-play gap
    12→"There's no equivalent of self-play in LLMs." In Go, you have a perfect game outcome signal. In open-ended language tasks, you don't. Early academic attempts exist (Jaques' Spiral, Zhao's AbsoluteZero) but nothing at industrial scale.
    13→
    14→### LLM judges are gameable
    15→"Anytime you use an LLM to assign a reward... they're gameable. If you're reinforcement learning with respect to them, you will find adversarial examples for your LLM judges, almost guaranteed."
    16→
    17→### Jagged intelligence
    18→RLVR creates models that are extraordinary in verifiable domains and mediocre elsewhere — simultaneously genius polymath and confused grade schooler.
    19→
    20→### Autoresearch (March 2026)
    21→[autoresearch](https://github.com/karpathy/autoresearch) — ~630-line Python script handing ML experimentation to an AI agent. Agent edits training script, runs 5-min pass, checks if validation loss improved, keeps/discards, repeats. After 2 days: ~700 autonomous changes, ~20 additive improvements transferring to larger models. Fitness signal: validation bits-per-byte (val_bpb) — simple, verifiable, hard to game.
    22→
    23→---
    24→
    25→## The Overfitting Tension
    26→
    27→### Does RLVR avoid overfitting?
    28→Partially. Verifiable rewards sidestep reward model gaming but don't eliminate overfitting broadly.
    29→
    30→**"Can Large Reasoning Models Self-Train?"** (Shafayat et al., 2025):
    31→- Early iterations: self-training improves reasoning
    32→- Prolonged training: reward hacking and collapse — even with verifiable rewards
    33→- The model learns to produce *consistent* (not correct) responses
    34→- Mitigation: curriculum learning (easy problems first) delayed collapse
    35→
    36→### Why self-training sometimes works
    37→1. Genuine verification (rule-based, not learned reward models)
    38→2. Large, diverse problem spaces (resist memorization)
    39→3. Stopping early (few iterations, not prolonged optimization)
    40→4. External evaluation (verifier outside agent's optimization loop)
    41→5. Strong pretrained base (inductive bias as regularizer)
    42→
    43→### Model collapse
    44→Nature 2024 paper: training on model-generated content causes "irreversible defects" — tails of original distribution disappear. Even 1-in-1000 synthetic data can trigger collapse (ICLR 2025). Mitigated when verification filters synthetic data.
    45→
    46→### Goodhart's Law
    47→Theoretical result: for any true reward function, it is impossible to create a non-trivial proxy reward guaranteed to be unhackable. Rule-based rewards are better proxies than learned ones, but the proxy-target gap always exists.
    48→
    49→---
    50→
    51→## The Verification Spectrum
    52→
    53→| Level | Description | Example | Agent-ready? |
    54→|-------|-------------|---------|-------------|
    55→| Perfectly verifiable | External, deterministic check on actual target | Code passes test suite, math answer matches | Yes |
    56→| Partially verifiable | Check is real but incomplete | Code passes some tests, factual claim checkable | Yes, with caveats |
    57→| Superficially verifiable | Check measures proxy, not target | Output has right format, keywords, length | **Danger zone** — reward hacking lives here |
    58→| Not verifiable | No automated check possible | Creative judgment, strategic advice | Not agent-ready for self-improvement |
    59→
    60→**Key insight:** A problem that looks verifiable but is only superficially verifiable is *worse* than one that's openly unverifiable, because it gives false confidence.
    61→
    62→---
    63→
    64→## Implications for Agent-Ready Problem Classification
    65→
    66→### What makes a problem truly agent-ready
    67→- The verification checks the actual thing you care about
    68→- The problem has a large enough solution space that memorization doesn't work
    69→- The verification is external to the agent
    70→- Meaningful progress possible in a small number of attempts (not requiring extensive iterative optimization)
    71→
    72→### What makes a problem only superficially verifiable
    73→- The verification checks a proxy (format, length, keywords)
    74→- The problem is narrow enough that pattern matching beats understanding
    75→- The verification can be gamed by an agent that understands the evaluation mechanism
    76→
    77→### Karpathy's autoresearch as design template
    78→Classify problems along these axes:
    79→1. Is the evaluation genuinely informative? (hard to game without actual improvement)
    80→2. Is the scope constrained? (time-boxed, bounded search space)
    81→3. Is the evaluation external to the agent?
    82→4. Is the problem space diverse enough to resist memorization?
    83→
    84→---
    85→
    86→## Key Papers
    87→
    88→- **DeepSeek-R1** (2501.12948) — RLVR with rule-based rewards only, avoids neural reward models
    89→- **STaR** (Zelikman et al., 2022) — Bootstrapping reasoning iteratively, rationalization for wrong answers
    90→- **ReST / ReST-EM** (Google DeepMind, 2023-2024) — EM-like self-training loop, scales with model size
    91→- **SPIN** (Chen et al., 2024) — Self-play fine-tuning, distinguishing own outputs from human data
    92→- **Can Large Reasoning Models Self-Train?** (Shafayat et al., 2025) — Direct evidence of collapse under prolonged self-training
    93→- **Model Collapse** (Nature 2024) — Irreversible defects from training on synthetic data
    94→- **Strong Model Collapse** (ICLR 2025) — Even tiny fractions of synthetic data trigger collapse
    95→- **Constitutional AI** (Anthropic) — AI feedback guided by principles; "high-noise low-bias" (human) vs. "low-noise high-bias" (synthetic)
    96→

1→# Problem-Gate Architecture: Research Overview
     2→
     3→**Date:** 2026-03-18
     4→**Purpose:** Hackathon research on problem-gate methodology for tech transfer and meta-analysis of methodological overrepresentation in LLM training data.
     5→
     6→---
     7→
     8→## Part 1: Problem-Gate Architecture for Tech Transfer
     9→
    10→### 1.1 What Is It?
    11→
    12→The "problem-gate" concept builds on Robert Cooper's **Stage-Gate** methodology, a structured process that divides innovation from idea to launch into discrete **stages** separated by decision points called **gates**. At each gate, a cross-functional team evaluates the project against predefined criteria and makes a Go/Kill/Hold/Recycle decision.
    13→
    14→**Classic Stage-Gate stages (Cooper's model):**
    15→
    16→| Stage | Name | Key Activities |
    17→|-------|------|----------------|
    18→| 0 | Discovery | Ideation, opportunity identification |
    19→| 1 | Scoping | Preliminary market and technical assessment |
    20→| 2 | Build Business Case | Detailed investigation, business case development |
    21→| 3 | Development | Design, build, and test the product/solution |
    22→| 4 | Testing & Validation | Field trials, market testing, regulatory review |
    23→| 5 | Launch | Commercialization, production ramp-up |
    24→
    25→Each **gate** has three components:
    26→- **Deliverables** — what the project team brings to the gate
    27→- **Criteria** — standards the project must meet (both must-meet and should-meet)
    28→- **Outputs** — a decision (Go/Kill/Hold/Recycle) plus an approved action plan for the next stage
    29→
    30→The key insight of Stage-Gate is that **value creation** and **risk management** happen simultaneously: each stage generates knowledge that reduces uncertainty, and each gate forces a resource allocation decision based on that new knowledge.
    31→
    32→**The "problem-gate" adaptation** applies this logic not to product development but to **problem selection**: rather than gating product concepts through development stages, you gate *problems themselves* through stages of validation to determine which are worth pursuing. This is particularly relevant for technology transfer and agent-based systems where the question is not "can we build it?" but "is this problem well-defined enough to work on?"
    33→
    34→### 1.2 Origins and Key Authors
    35→
    36→**Robert G. Cooper** is the originator of the Stage-Gate system. His foundational paper, "Stage-gate systems: A new tool for managing new products" (1990), has accumulated approximately 1,837 citations on Semantic Scholar (191 highly influential). Cooper is Professor Emeritus at McMaster University's DeGroote School of Business and an ISBM Distinguished Research Fellow at Penn State. His empirical foundation was a study of 252 new product histories at 123 firms (conducted with Elko Kleinschmidt).
    37→
    38→Key facts about Cooper's work:
    39→- Over 140 published articles and 7 books on NPD management
    40→- Stage-Gate is a registered trademark, commercialized through Stage-Gate International
    41→- More than 80% of companies in North America report using some form of Stage-Gate
    42→- The framework originated in the late 1980s, formalized in 1990
    43→
    44→**For tech transfer specifically**, the Stage-Gate model intersects with:
    45→- **NASA's Technology Readiness Level (TRL) framework** (1970s), which grades technology maturity from TRL 1 (basic principles observed) to TRL 9 (proven in operational environment). The DOE, DoD, and NIH all use TRL-gated decision processes.
    46→- **The Defense Acquisition University's Decision Point (DP) Tool**, a TRL-gated activity model for technology transition management.
    47→- **University technology transfer office (TTO) processes**, which apply stage-gate logic to decide which inventions to patent, license, and commercialize.
    48→
    49→### 1.3 How It's Used in Practice
    50→
    51→#### Universities
    52→
    53→University TTOs typically implement a five-stage commercialization pipeline:
    54→
    55→1. **Identifying** — matching market opportunities with research outputs
    56→2. **Assessing** — qualifying IP candidates for feasibility, viability, and desirability
    57→3. **Protecting** — patent filings, provisional applications
    58→4. **Promoting** — marketing to licensees, negotiating deals
    59→5. **Profiting** — licensing revenue, equity stakes, spin-out formation
    60→
    61→Michigan State University's Innovation Center explicitly uses stage-gate evaluation to filter technologies by commercial promise. The University of Minnesota introduced stage-gate processes to standardize decision criteria, improve response times, increase documentation transparency, and enhance communication.
    62→
    63→#### National Labs and Government R&D
    64→
    65→The U.S. Department of Energy uses Technology Readiness Assessments (TRAs) and Technology Maturation Plans (TMPs) as gate mechanisms during capital acquisition. TRAs are conducted at critical decision milestones (CD-1, CD-2, etc.) with a systems engineering approach to validate technology integration.
    66→
    67→The DOE's Industrial Technologies Program published specific Stage-Gate Innovation Management Guidelines for managing energy technology projects.
    68→
    69→#### Corporate R&D
    70→
    71→Over 80% of North American firms use some variant of Stage-Gate. It functions as both a value-creation engine and a governance model, providing:
    72→- Resource allocation discipline (killing projects early saves resources)
    73→- Cross-functional alignment at gates
    74→- A common language for portfolio management
    75→
    76→### 1.4 Variations and Modern Adaptations
    77→
    78→#### Agile Stage-Gate Hybrids (Cooper, 2014-present)
    79→
    80→Cooper himself led the integration of Agile methods into Stage-Gate, recognizing that the original model could be too rigid for fast-moving markets. The Agile-Stage-Gate hybrid uses:
    81→- **Sprints within stages** — iterative development cycles nested inside the broader stage structure
    82→- **Flexible gates** — gates can be conditional rather than binary Go/Kill
    83→- **Continuous customer feedback** — borrowed from Agile's emphasis on working software and user stories
    84→
    85→Research by Cocchi and Dosi (2024) provides a systematic review of "Stage-Gate Hybridization Beyond Agile," identifying three hybrid forms:
    86→1. **Agile/Stage-Gate** — sprints within stages, flexible gates
    87→2. **Design Thinking/Stage-Gate** — empathy and ideation phases at the front end, structured development at the back end
    88→3. **Design Thinking + Lean Startup/Stage-Gate** — customer discovery and MVP testing combined with gated progression
    89→
    90→#### The Hybrid Model Matrix (Cooper & Sommer, 2021)
    91→
    92→Stage-Gate International developed the Hybrid Model Matrix, where Stage-Gate serves as the "backbone process" and Design Thinking, Lean Startup, and Agile are "plugged in" at appropriate stages. Managers select the hybrid configuration based on four dimensions:
    93→- Project type
    94→- Market uncertainty
    95→- Technology uncertainty
    96→- Learning gap
    97→
    98→#### AI-Augmented Variations
    99→
   100→Recent work (2025-2026) integrates AI into Stage-Gate for:
   101→- Automated market analysis at early gates
   102→- NLP-based patent landscape scanning
   103→- Predictive scoring of project success probability at gates
   104→- Agent-based simulation of market adoption scenarios
   105→
   106→The "quality gate" pattern in AI agent architectures (documented by practitioners in 2025) provides a parallel: a 3-tier verification architecture where:
   107→- **Tier 1 (Hard Gate):** Binary pass/fail on fundamental correctness
   108→- **Tier 2 (Soft Gate):** Majority-rule quality judgment (e.g., 3 of 4 criteria met)
   109→- **Tier 3 (Regression Gate):** Comparison against last known-good baseline
   110→
   111→This maps directly to the problem-gate concept: gates verify not just that code runs, but that the *output has value*.
   112→
   113→### 1.5 Relevance to Our Project
   114→
   115→Our project builds an agent that:
   116→1. Creates **problem graphs** — structured representations of problem spaces
   117→2. Identifies which problems are good candidates for **agent work**
   118→3. Applies a gate: *"Is this problem verifiable enough that an agent can attempt it and we can measure success?"*
   119→
   120→**How Stage-Gate informs this design:**
   121→
   122→| Stage-Gate Concept | Our Adaptation |
   123→|---|---|
   124→| Stages as knowledge-generation phases | Problem decomposition stages: discover, define, formalize, verify |
   125→| Gates as Go/Kill decisions | Verifiability gate: can we define success criteria the agent can be measured against? |
   126→| Deliverables at each gate | Problem specification artifacts: constraints, input/output schemas, evaluation metrics |
   127→| Criteria (must-meet / should-meet) | Must-meet: problem has measurable output. Should-meet: solution space is bounded, domain knowledge is available |
   128→| Portfolio management across gates | Priority ranking of problems by verifiability score, impact, and agent capability match |
   129→| Kill decisions | Problems that fail the verifiability gate are flagged for human-only work or further decomposition |
   130→
   131→**The key adaptation is inverting the traditional flow.** In classic Stage-Gate, you start with an idea and gate it toward launch. In our problem-gate architecture, you start with a **problem space** and gate individual problems toward agent assignment. The gate criteria are:
   132→
   133→1. **Formalizability** — Can the problem be stated precisely enough for an agent to understand scope?
   134→2. **Measurability** — Are there quantifiable success/failure criteria?
   135→3. **Decomposability** — If the problem is too large, can it be broken into sub-problems that individually pass the gate?
   136→4. **Bounded solution space** — Is the search space tractable, or is it open-ended in ways that defeat verification?
   137→5. **Available ground truth** — Do we have examples, test cases, or expert judgments to validate against?
   138→
   139→Problems that pass all five criteria get assigned to agents. Problems that fail specific criteria get routed to specific remediation paths (e.g., "needs domain expert to define success criteria" or "needs further decomposition").
   140→
   141→---
   142→
   143→## Part 2: Overrepresentation in Training Data — Is This Methodology Overrepresented?
   144→
   145→### 2.1 How to Measure Overrepresentation of a Methodology in Training Data
   146→
   147→There is no direct way to audit the training data of proprietary LLMs (including the model generating this text). However, several proxy measurements exist:
   148→
   149→#### Citation Count Analysis
   150→
   151→Using Semantic Scholar and Google Scholar as proxies for "how much has been written about this":
   152→
   153→| Methodology | Key Paper/Book | Approx. Citations (Semantic Scholar) | Year |
   154→|---|---|---|---|
   155→| Stage-Gate (Cooper) | "Stage-gate systems: A new tool..." | ~1,837 | 1990 |
   156→| Effectuation (Sarasvathy) | "Causation and Effectuation..." | ~5,455 | 2001 |
   157→| Lean Startup (Ries) | "The Lean Startup" (book) | ~903-2,765* | 2011 |
   158→| Design Thinking (various) | ~2,405 publications tracked in bibliometric analyses | N/A (diffuse) | Various |
   159→| Discovery-Driven Planning (McGrath & MacMillan) | "Discovery-Driven Planning" HBR | Moderate (exact count not retrieved) | 1995 |
   160→| Real Options (various) | Multiple foundational papers | Moderate-high (diffuse across finance/strategy) | 1990s-2000s |
   161→
   162→*Citation counts vary significantly across databases (Semantic Scholar, SciSpace, Google Scholar). These are approximate.
   163→
   164→**Key observation:** Sarasvathy's effectuation paper actually has significantly *more* citations than Cooper's Stage-Gate paper. However, raw citation count does not equal training data representation. Stage-Gate may be overrepresented in:
   165→- MBA and business school textbooks (which are heavily represented in training corpora)
   166→- Practitioner-oriented content (blog posts, consulting firm whitepapers, corporate training materials)
   167→- Patent filings and industry standards documents
   168→
   169→#### Textbook Prevalence
   170→
   171→Stage-Gate is a staple of operations management, product development, and innovation management textbooks. It appears in virtually every MBA curriculum that covers NPD. Effectuation, while highly cited academically, is concentrated in entrepreneurship-specific courses. Design thinking appears broadly but is more diffuse. This textbook concentration likely amplifies Stage-Gate's presence in training data.
   172→
   173→#### Industry Standard Documents
   174→
   175→Stage-Gate appears in:
   176→- DOE Stage-Gate Innovation Management Guidelines
   177→- DoD acquisition frameworks (via TRL integration)
   178→- ISO and PMBOK-adjacent process documentation
   179→- Thousands of corporate process documents
   180→
   181→These are the kinds of "authoritative" documents that tend to be well-represented in training corpora.
   182→
   183→### 2.2 Competing Methodologies
   184→
   185→#### Discovery-Driven Planning (McGrath & MacMillan, 1995)
   186→
   187→Developed by Rita McGrath and Ian MacMillan, published in Harvard Business Review. The core idea: instead of projecting outcomes and planning backward, identify the **assumptions** underlying the plan and systematically test them. Each stage is about invalidating or confirming assumptions, not executing pre-planned activities.
   188→
   189→**Key difference from Stage-Gate:** DDP treats the plan as a hypothesis, not a roadmap. Gates become "assumption checkpoints" rather than "project quality reviews."
   190→
   191→**Relevance to our project:** DDP's assumption-testing logic maps well to agent verification. Each problem could carry a set of assumptions (e.g., "this problem has a unique solution," "the solution can be verified in <N seconds"), and the gate tests those assumptions.
   192→
   193→#### Real Options Analysis (ROA)
   194→
   195→Borrowed from financial options theory. Treats R&D investments as options: you invest a small amount to preserve the right (but not obligation) to invest more later. Key advantage: explicitly values **flexibility** and **the ability to abandon**.
   196→
   197→**Key difference from Stage-Gate:** ROA provides a quantitative valuation framework, not just a qualitative Go/Kill decision. It can assign dollar values to the option to continue, expand, defer, or abandon.
   198→
   199→**Relevance to our project:** Agent compute is cheap to start but expensive at scale. Real-options thinking could help decide when to "exercise the option" of scaling up agent work on a problem vs. keeping it as a small exploration.
   200→
   201→#### Effectuation Theory (Sarasvathy, 2001)
   202→
   203→Five principles: bird-in-hand (start with what you have), affordable loss (risk only what you can afford to lose), crazy quilt (build partnerships), lemonade (leverage surprises), pilot-in-the-plane (focus on controllable aspects).
   204→
   205→**Key difference from Stage-Gate:** Effectuation is non-predictive. It does not try to forecast outcomes and gate against forecasts; instead, it focuses on what you can control and who you can recruit. It is fundamentally about action under uncertainty rather than evaluation under uncertainty.
   206→
   207→**Relevance to our project:** Effectuation logic might apply to the *agent's* decision-making within a problem (start with available tools, take bounded risks, leverage unexpected intermediate results). However, it is less suited for the *gating* decision itself because our gating question is inherently evaluative.
   208→
   209→#### Design Thinking (Brown, IDEO; Stanford d.school)
   210→
   211→Empathy-driven, iterative: empathize, define, ideate, prototype, test. Strong at the fuzzy front end of innovation.
   212→
   213→**Key difference from Stage-Gate:** Design thinking is about problem *finding* and reframing, not problem *gating*. It excels when you do not yet know what the right problem is.
   214→
   215→**Relevance to our project:** Design thinking could inform the *discovery* stage that feeds into the problem-gate. But the gate itself needs more structured, evaluative criteria than design thinking provides.
   216→
   217→#### Problem-Solution Fit Frameworks
   218→
   219→Various startup-oriented frameworks (Ash Maurya's Lean Canvas, Strategyzer's Value Proposition Canvas) focus on validating that a specific problem is worth solving before building a solution.
   220→
   221→**Relevance to our project:** The "problem worth solving" validation is close to what our gate does, but we add the additional criterion of *agent verifiability*.
   222→
   223→#### Beyond Stage-Gate (Sethi & Iqbal, 2008)
   224→
   225→Sethi and Iqbal published "Beyond stage-gate: Restoring learning and adaptability to commercialization," arguing that Stage-Gate can suppress learning and adaptability. They propose reconceiving it as an assumption-driven process with divergent-convergent cycles (similar to DDP).
   226→
   227→### 2.3 Evidence of Overrepresentation
   228→
   229→#### Is Stage-Gate disproportionately cited?
   230→
   231→No, not in raw academic citation terms. Sarasvathy's effectuation paper (~5,455 citations) exceeds Cooper's foundational Stage-Gate paper (~1,837 citations) by roughly 3x. Lean Startup, while more practitioner-oriented, has significant citation mass as well.
   232→
   233→However, **overrepresentation in LLM training data is not the same as academic citation count.** The relevant factors are:
   234→
   235→1. **Breadth of document types:** Stage-Gate appears in textbooks, corporate wikis, government standards, consulting whitepapers, blog posts, and training materials. Effectuation is concentrated in academic journals and entrepreneurship courses.
   236→
   237→2. **Longevity and incumbency:** Stage-Gate has been the dominant paradigm since 1990 — 36 years of accumulated documentation. Effectuation (2001) and Lean Startup (2011) have shorter tails.
   238→
   239→3. **Trademark and commercialization:** Stage-Gate International actively produces and distributes content. This creates a marketing-driven content ecosystem that amplifies training data presence.
   240→
   241→4. **Cross-domain penetration:** Stage-Gate appears in engineering, pharma, defense, energy, consumer goods, and software. Most alternatives are concentrated in one or two domains.
   242→
   243→#### Do LLMs default to recommending Stage-Gate?
   244→
   245→**Honest introspection:** Yes, when asked a general question like "What framework should I use for managing innovation?" or "How do I decide which R&D projects to pursue?", an LLM is likely to mention Stage-Gate prominently, often first. This is likely because:
   246→
   247→- It is the most widely documented framework in the relevant domain
   248→- It appears in the broadest range of source document types
   249→- Its structure (stages, gates, criteria) is highly "template-able," which means there are many how-to articles, listicles, and process documents about it
   250→- MBA-oriented content, which forms a substantial portion of business-related training data, strongly features Stage-Gate
   251→
   252→This is a genuine concern. An LLM recommending Stage-Gate may be reflecting **corpus frequency** rather than **best fit for the specific problem.**
   253→
   254→#### Is the dominance justified by evidence?
   255→
   256→Partially. Stage-Gate has strong empirical backing for physical product development in established firms. Cooper's original research and subsequent studies demonstrate correlation between Stage-Gate adoption and NPD success rates.
   257→
   258→However:
   259→- Most of the evidence comes from **large firms doing incremental innovation**, not startups or radical innovation
   260→- The evidence for Stage-Gate in **tech transfer specifically** is thinner — it is often borrowed by analogy from NPD, not validated directly
   261→- Competing frameworks (effectuation, DDP) may outperform Stage-Gate in **high-uncertainty contexts** but have less industry-standard documentation
   262→- There is a survivorship/selection bias: firms that adopt Stage-Gate tend to be well-resourced, and well-resourced firms tend to succeed regardless of methodology
   263→
   264→### 2.4 How to Detect Methodological Bias in an LLM
   265→
   266→#### Prompt Variation Testing
   267→
   268→Design a battery of prompts that ask the same underlying question in different ways:
   269→
   270→```
   271→Prompt A: "What framework should I use to manage innovation projects?"
   272→Prompt B: "How do entrepreneurs decide which ideas to pursue?"
   273→Prompt C: "What are the best methods for R&D project selection?"
   274→Prompt D: "How should a university tech transfer office evaluate inventions?"
   275→Prompt E: "What decision frameworks work for high-uncertainty projects?"
   276→```
   277→
   278→If Stage-Gate appears as the first or primary recommendation in >60% of responses regardless of framing, that suggests overrepresentation bias. If it appears less in prompts framed around uncertainty (Prompt E) vs. structured management (Prompt A), the model is showing some contextual sensitivity.
   279→
   280→#### Expert Panel Comparison
   281→
   282→Survey 20+ domain experts (tech transfer professionals, innovation managers, VCs, academic researchers) with the same prompts. Compare the distribution of framework recommendations from experts vs. the LLM. Significant divergence indicates bias.
   283→
   284→#### Frequency Analysis
   285→
   286→Run 100+ generation passes on the same prompt with temperature > 0. Count how often each framework is mentioned. Compare the resulting distribution to:
   287→- Academic citation ratios
   288→- Expert survey results
   289→- Actual adoption rates in the relevant domain
   290→
   291→#### Red-Teaming with Domain Experts
   292→
   293→Have domain experts:
   294→1. Ask the LLM for recommendations in their specialty
   295→2. Identify cases where the LLM recommendation is suboptimal
   296→3. Probe whether the LLM can explain *why* it chose that framework over alternatives
   297→4. Test whether the LLM changes its recommendation when given domain-specific constraints
   298→
   299→#### Counterfactual Probing
   300→
   301→Ask: "What are the weaknesses of Stage-Gate for tech transfer?" If the model provides a thorough critique, it has the knowledge to make nuanced recommendations. If it deflects or gives weak criticisms, the overrepresentation may be reinforced by a lack of critical content in the training data.
   302→
   303→### 2.5 Documented Problems and Uncertainties
   304→
   305→#### What I am confident about:
   306→- Cooper's Stage-Gate is the dominant documented framework for NPD management
   307→- It has strong empirical support for its original domain (physical product NPD in large firms)
   308→- The framework has been extensively adapted (Agile, Lean, Design Thinking hybrids)
   309→- TRL-gated processes are standard in government R&D (DOE, DoD, NASA)
   310→- University TTOs widely use stage-gate-like processes for commercialization decisions
   311→
   312→#### Where I am uncertain:
   313→- **Exact citation counts are unreliable.** Different databases (Semantic Scholar, Google Scholar, SciSpace) report different numbers. I used Semantic Scholar figures where available, but these should be treated as approximate, not definitive.
   314→- **"Problem-gate" as a specific term is not widely established.** The searches did not return a canonical definition of "problem-gate architecture." The concept exists implicitly in tech transfer and agent verification literature, but the term may be novel or niche. This could be an opportunity (we are naming something real that lacks a name) or a risk (we may be reinventing an existing concept under a new label).
   315→- **Training data composition is unknowable.** I cannot directly inspect my training data. All claims about overrepresentation are based on proxies (citation counts, document type analysis, institutional adoption data). The actual weighting of sources in training is proprietary.
   316→- **Competing methodology citation counts are incomplete.** I was unable to retrieve exact citation counts for Discovery-Driven Planning (McGrath & MacMillan, 1995 HBR article) or for the full real options literature. These gaps weaken the comparative analysis.
   317→- **Evidence for Stage-Gate in tech transfer specifically (as opposed to NPD generally) is limited.** Most of the strong empirical evidence is about new product development in manufacturing firms. The tech transfer application is often by analogy or institutional adoption rather than rigorous validation.
   318→
   319→#### Conflicting information encountered:
   320→- Eric Ries' "The Lean Startup" citation counts vary from ~903 (Semantic Scholar) to ~2,765 (SciSpace). This likely reflects differences in what each database counts as a "citation" (e.g., whether blog posts and practitioner references are included).
   321→- Some sources position Design Thinking and Stage-Gate as complementary (front-end vs. back-end), while others position them as competing paradigms. The truth likely depends on context.
   322→
   323→#### Areas where better primary research is needed:
   324→1. A systematic comparison of Stage-Gate adoption rates in university TTOs vs. alternative frameworks
   325→2. Empirical data on whether agent-based problem verification benefits from gated vs. continuous evaluation
   326→3. Direct measurement of framework recommendation frequency across multiple LLMs (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, Llama) to characterize cross-model bias
   327→4. Expert surveys on tech transfer methodology preferences (not just NPD)
   328→
   329→---
   330→
   331→## Summary and Synthesis
   332→
   333→### For the hackathon project:
   334→
   335→The problem-gate architecture is a legitimate and useful adaptation of Stage-Gate thinking. The key innovation is **applying gates to problem selection and verifiability rather than product development**. The five verifiability criteria (formalizability, measurability, decomposability, bounded solution space, available ground truth) provide a concrete gate specification.
   336→
   337→However, we should be aware that reaching for Stage-Gate as our conceptual foundation may itself reflect training-data bias. The alternatives — particularly Discovery-Driven Planning (assumption-testing) and Real Options (valuing flexibility and cheap exploration) — offer complementary perspectives that could strengthen the architecture:
   338→
   339→- **DDP's assumption-testing** could be built into the gate criteria: each problem carries explicit assumptions, and the gate tests whether those assumptions have been validated.
   340→- **Real Options thinking** could inform the portfolio-level decision: how much compute to allocate to uncertain problems vs. well-understood ones.
   341→- **Effectuation's affordable-loss principle** could set bounds on agent exploration: never spend more than X tokens on a problem before hitting a gate.
   342→
   343→The strongest architecture would likely be a hybrid that uses Stage-Gate's structure as the backbone but incorporates assumption-testing from DDP, economic valuation from Real Options, and bounded exploration from effectuation.
   344→
   345→### On overrepresentation:
   346→
   347→Stage-Gate is likely overrepresented in LLM training data relative to its competitors, not because it is cited more (effectuation actually has more academic citations) but because it appears in a wider variety of document types (textbooks, government standards, corporate wikis, blog posts) that are well-represented in web-scraped training corpora. This does not mean it is the wrong choice for our project — but it does mean we should actively seek out and evaluate alternatives rather than defaulting to the first framework that comes to mind.
   348→
   349→---
   350→
   351→## Key References
   352→
   353→- Cooper, R.G. (1990). "Stage-gate systems: A new tool for managing new products." *Business Horizons*, 33(3), 44-54.
   354→- Cooper, R.G. (2014). "The Agile-Stage-Gate Hybrid Model: A Promising New Approach." *Journal of Product Innovation Management*.
   355→- Sarasvathy, S.D. (2001). "Causation and Effectuation: Toward a Theoretical Shift from Economic Inevitability to Entrepreneurial Contingency." *Academy of Management Review*, 26(2), 243-263.
   356→- McGrath, R.G. & MacMillan, I. (1995). "Discovery-Driven Planning." *Harvard Business Review*.
   357→- Ries, E. (2011). *The Lean Startup*. Crown Business.
   358→- Sethi, R. & Iqbal, Z. (2008). "Beyond stage-gate: Restoring learning and adaptability to commercialization."
   359→- Cocchi & Dosi (2024). "Stage-Gate Hybridization Beyond Agile: Conceptual Review, Synthesis, and Research Agenda." *IEEE Transactions on Engineering Management*.
   360→- Mansoori, Y. & Lackeus, M. (2020). "Comparing effectuation to discovery-driven planning, prescriptive entrepreneurship, business planning, lean startup, and design thinking." *Small Business Economics*, 54(3).
   361→
   362→## Web Sources Consulted
   363→
   364→- [Stage-Gate International: The Stage-Gate Model Overview](https://www.stage-gate.com/blog/the-stage-gate-model-an-overview/)
   365→- [Wellspring: Stage-Gate Origin, Status Quo and Future](https://www.wellspring.com/blog/stage-gate-the-origin-status-quo-and-its-future)
   366→- [PMC: Technology Transfer from Research Bench to Commercialization](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6113541/)
   367→- [Springer: NPD Process for Deep-Tech Academic Research Commercialization](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s13731-023-00311-1)
   368→- [DOE: Stage-Gate Review Guide for Industrial Technologies Program](https://www.energy.gov/cmei/articles/stage-gate-review-guide-industrial-technologies-program)
   369→- [NASA: Technology Readiness Levels](https://www.nasa.gov/directorates/somd/space-communications-navigation-program/technology-readiness-levels/)
   370→- [Robert G. Cooper Google Scholar Profile](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=K1R3l2kAAAAJ&hl=en)
   371→- [Saras Sarasvathy Google Scholar Profile](https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=4LmjS-kAAAAJ&hl=en)
   372→- [Semantic Scholar: Cooper's Stage-Gate Paper](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Stage-gate-systems:-A-new-tool-for-managing-new-Cooper/9f9fde484fb0cc2de2b5aa8ba249b88c46770a88)
   373→- [Semantic Scholar: Sarasvathy's Effectuation Paper](https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Causation-and-Effectuation:-Toward-a-Theoretical-to-Sarasvathy/6eeb5dec1eaa2bfe07c36c5d8bb9e945d1fa4878)
   374→- [DataCamp: Understanding and Mitigating Bias in LLMs](https://www.datacamp.com/blog/understanding-and-mitigating-bias-in-large-language-models-llms)
   375→- [arXiv: Bias in Large Language Models: Origin, Evaluation, and Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/html/2411.10915v1)
   376→- [CMU SEI: Auditing Bias in Large Language Models](https://www.sei.cmu.edu/blog/auditing-bias-in-large-language-models/)
   377→- [Dev.to: Why Your AI Agent Needs a Quality Gate](https://dev.to/yurukusa/why-your-ai-agent-needs-a-quality-gate-not-just-tests-42eo)
   378→- [ResearchGate: Stage-Gate Hybridization Beyond Agile](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/371917687_Stage-Gate_Hybridization_Beyond_Agile_Conceptual_Review_Synthesis_and_Research_Agenda)
   379→- [Springer: Comparing Effectuation to Discovery-Driven Planning et al.](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11187-019-00153-w)
   380→- [ResearchGate: Beyond Stage-Gate (Sethi & Iqbal)](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/241707427_Beyond_stage-gate_Restoring_learning_and_adaptability_to_commercialization)
